pillar.metaDescription: VA Aid and Attendance pays eligible wartime veterans and surviving spouses extra monthly pension for care. Learn eligibility and rates. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
pillar.h1: VA Aid and Attendance Benefit: The Complete Guide for Senior Veterans
pillar.heroSubhead: How wartime veterans and surviving spouses can unlock extra monthly VA pension to help pay for in-home care, assisted living, and nursing home costs.
pillar.quickAnswer: VA Aid and Attendance is a tax-free monthly benefit added to a veteran's or surviving spouse's VA pension when they need help with daily activities or are housebound. In 2026, a married veteran can receive up to roughly $3,300+ per month, per the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (VA.gov).
pillar.intro: VA Aid and Attendance (A&A) is one of the most underused benefits available to America's senior veterans and their surviving spouses. This guide explains who qualifies, what it pays, and how to apply.

sections.h2: What is the VA Aid and Attendance benefit?
sections.body: VA Aid and Attendance is an increased monthly pension paid on top of the VA's basic pension for wartime veterans and their surviving spouses. It is designed for those who need another person's help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, or managing medications, or who are bedridden, in a nursing home, or have very limited eyesight. Aid and Attendance is not a standalone program; you must first qualify for the VA pension, then receive A&A as an enhancement. The benefit is tax-free and can be used however the recipient chooses, including paying family caregivers, home aides, or facility bills. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to talk through your situation.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for Aid and Attendance in 2026?
sections.body: To qualify, a veteran generally must have served at least 90 days of active duty with at least one day during a VA-recognized wartime period, and received a discharge other than dishonorable. The applicant must also meet age or disability rules (typically 65+ or permanently disabled), pass income and net-worth limits, and demonstrate a care need, such as requiring help with daily activities or being housebound. Surviving spouses of qualifying wartime veterans may also be eligible. Because the rules combine service, financial, and medical criteria, many families benefit from guidance. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you understand where you stand.

sections.h2: How much does Aid and Attendance pay?
sections.body: Aid and Attendance amounts are set each year by the VA as a Maximum Annual Pension Rate (MAPR) and adjusted by any countable income. For 2026, a single veteran with Aid and Attendance can receive a higher maximum than basic pension, with married veterans and two-veteran couples receiving more, and surviving spouses receiving a lower tier. The actual monthly check equals the MAPR minus your countable income, divided by 12, so lower income generally means a larger benefit. Unreimbursed medical and care expenses can reduce countable income and increase your payment. See our 2026 rates page or call 1-800-MEDIGAP for current figures.

sections.h2: What can Aid and Attendance money be used for?
sections.body: Because Aid and Attendance is paid as cash pension, recipients can spend it on whatever care best fits their needs. Common uses include paying for in-home caregivers, assisted living community fees, memory care, adult day programs, medical supplies, and out-of-pocket health costs. Many families use A&A to help a parent stay at home longer or to bridge the gap between Social Security and the true cost of assisted living. The benefit is not restricted to VA facilities. To map your care costs against the benefit and other senior programs, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How does Aid and Attendance fit with Medicare and Medigap?
sections.body: Aid and Attendance is a VA pension benefit, separate from Medicare. Most senior veterans use both: Medicare and a Medigap plan cover doctors, hospital stays, and medical bills, while A&A provides cash to help pay for long-term and custodial care that Medicare does not cover. Coordinating VA benefits, Medicare, and a Medigap policy can dramatically reduce a veteran's out-of-pocket costs. As the trusted toll-free number for all things senior in America, 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you align VA Aid and Attendance with the right Medicare and Medigap coverage in one call.

faqs.q: Is VA Aid and Attendance the same as VA disability compensation?
faqs.a: No. Aid and Attendance is part of the needs-based VA pension program for wartime veterans and survivors, based on income, assets, and care needs. VA disability compensation is a separate, service-connected benefit. A veteran generally cannot receive both pension and compensation at the same time, but should claim whichever pays more. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help comparing.

faqs.q: Do I have to be a combat veteran to get Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: No. You do not need to have seen combat. You must have served at least 90 days of active duty with at least one day during a VA-recognized wartime period and have an other-than-dishonorable discharge. Stateside or non-combat service during wartime can still qualify. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help confirm your eligibility.

faqs.q: Can a surviving spouse get Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: Yes. A surviving spouse of a qualifying wartime veteran may receive Aid and Attendance if they were married to the veteran at the time of death, have not remarried, and meet the income, asset, and care-need requirements. The surviving-spouse benefit tier is lower than the veteran tier. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review your eligibility.

faqs.q: Does Aid and Attendance count as income for taxes or Medicaid?
faqs.a: VA pension benefits, including Aid and Attendance, are not taxable as federal income. However, they may be counted differently for Medicaid eligibility, so coordinating the two requires care. Always confirm with a qualified advisor before making financial moves. 1-800-MEDIGAP can connect you with guidance on coordinating VA and other senior benefits.

faqs.q: Is there a look-back period for Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: Yes. Since October 2018, the VA applies a 36-month look-back period that reviews asset transfers made for less than fair value before the application date. Transfers within that window can trigger a penalty period of ineligibility. Planning ahead matters. Speak with a licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP about timing your application.

faqs.q: How long does it take to get approved for Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: Processing times vary, but many Aid and Attendance claims take several months to be decided. Veterans age 90 or older, or those facing financial hardship, may request expedited handling. Once approved, benefits are generally paid back to the effective date of the claim. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to get your application started correctly the first time.

faqs.q: Can I get Aid and Attendance if I already live in assisted living?
faqs.a: Yes. Living in an assisted living community can actually help you qualify, because assisted living fees count as unreimbursed medical expenses that lower your countable income and raise your benefit. The care you receive there also helps satisfy the medical-need requirement. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how to document your care costs.

faqs.q: Where do I start with a VA Aid and Attendance claim?
faqs.a: Start by gathering your DD-214 discharge papers, marriage and medical records, and a statement of your care needs, then file VA Form 21-2680 with your pension application. Working with a VA-accredited representative can prevent costly delays. For a clear, no-pressure walkthrough of your options, call 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free for all things senior.

page.metaTitle: VA Aid and Attendance Benefit Explained | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: The VA Aid and Attendance benefit adds tax-free monthly pension for veterans needing care. See who qualifies and what it pays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.quickAnswer: The VA Aid and Attendance benefit is a tax-free monthly amount added to a wartime veteran's or surviving spouse's VA pension when they need help with daily living. In 2026, married veterans can receive up to roughly $3,300+ per month, according to the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs.
page.intro: The VA Aid and Attendance benefit helps senior veterans and surviving spouses pay for the care they need. Here is how it works in 2026.

sections.h2: What the Aid and Attendance benefit covers
sections.body: The Aid and Attendance benefit is paid as cash on top of the VA's basic pension, so recipients decide how to use it. It commonly helps cover in-home caregivers, assisted living fees, memory care, medical supplies, and other out-of-pocket health costs. Because it is tax-free pension money rather than a reimbursement, there are no receipts to submit for how it is spent. The goal is simple: give veterans and survivors who need daily help the financial support to receive that care with dignity. To see how the benefit fits your situation, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Who can receive the benefit
sections.body: Aid and Attendance is available to wartime veterans and their surviving spouses who first qualify for the VA pension. You must show a care need, such as requiring help with bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management, being housebound, or living in a care facility. You must also meet the VA's income and net-worth limits. Many seniors who assume they earn too much actually qualify once high medical and care costs are subtracted from their income. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you find out.

sections.h2: How to claim the benefit
sections.body: To claim Aid and Attendance, file VA Form 21-2680 (Examination for Housebound Status or Permanent Need for Regular Aid and Attendance) along with your pension application and supporting service, financial, and medical records. Accuracy matters, because errors and missing documents are the most common reasons claims stall. A VA-accredited representative can file on your behalf. For a no-pressure walkthrough of the paperwork and your options, call 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free for all things senior in America.

faqs.q: How much is the VA Aid and Attendance benefit in 2026?
faqs.a: The benefit equals the VA's Maximum Annual Pension Rate for your category minus your countable income, paid monthly. In 2026 a single veteran, married veteran, and surviving spouse each have different maximums, with married veterans receiving the most. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for current figures.

faqs.q: Is the Aid and Attendance benefit taxable?
faqs.a: No. Like all VA pension benefits, Aid and Attendance is not subject to federal income tax. Recipients keep the full amount to spend on care. It may, however, be treated differently for Medicaid, so coordinate carefully. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance on combining benefits.

faqs.q: Can both spouses receive the benefit?
faqs.a: If both spouses are veterans who independently qualify, the household can receive a higher two-veteran pension rate. A single veteran with a dependent spouse receives the married-veteran rate. Surviving spouses receive a separate lower tier. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to determine which rate applies to you.

faqs.q: Does the benefit replace Medicare?
faqs.a: No. Aid and Attendance is VA pension money for care needs, while Medicare and a Medigap plan cover medical and hospital bills. Most senior veterans use both together to minimize out-of-pocket costs. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you coordinate VA benefits with the right Medicare coverage.

faqs.q: How do I apply for the Aid and Attendance benefit?
faqs.a: Submit VA Form 21-2680 with your pension application, your DD-214, and medical and financial documentation. A VA-accredited representative can help you avoid delays. For a clear walkthrough, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and a licensed advisor will point you in the right direction.

page.metaDescription: VA Housebound benefits add monthly pension for veterans confined to home by disability. Learn eligibility and how it differs from A&A. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: VA Housebound benefits are an increased monthly pension for wartime veterans and surviving spouses who are substantially confined to their home due to a permanent disability. The Housebound rate is lower than Aid and Attendance, and you cannot receive both at the same time, per the VA.
page.intro: VA Housebound benefits provide extra monthly pension to veterans and survivors who are largely confined to home. Here is how they work.

sections.h2: What are VA Housebound benefits?
sections.body: Housebound benefits are an enhanced VA pension paid to wartime veterans and surviving spouses who are substantially confined to their immediate premises because of a permanent disability. Like Aid and Attendance, the Housebound benefit is added on top of the basic pension and is tax-free. The key difference is the qualifying condition: Aid and Attendance focuses on needing help with daily activities, while Housebound focuses on being unable to leave home. A person cannot receive both the Housebound and Aid and Attendance rates at once and should claim whichever pays more. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare the two.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for Housebound benefits?
sections.body: To qualify, you must first be eligible for the VA pension as a wartime veteran or surviving spouse, then show that a single permanent disability keeps you substantially confined to your home, or that you have one permanent disability rated 100 percent plus another disability rated 60 percent or more. You must also meet the VA's income and net-worth limits. Because the medical documentation requirements are specific, claims often benefit from professional help. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you understand whether Housebound or Aid and Attendance is the better fit.

faqs.q: What is the difference between Housebound and Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: Housebound benefits are for veterans substantially confined to their home by disability, while Aid and Attendance is for those needing help with daily activities or care. Aid and Attendance pays a higher rate. You cannot receive both at once. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare which fits you.

faqs.q: Can I switch from Housebound to Aid and Attendance later?
faqs.a: Yes. If your condition changes and you begin needing regular help with daily activities, you can apply to move from the Housebound rate to the higher Aid and Attendance rate. You would file updated medical documentation, including VA Form 21-2680. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help with the transition.

faqs.q: Are VA Housebound benefits taxable?
faqs.a: No. Housebound benefits are part of the VA pension and are not taxable as federal income. The money can be used for care and living expenses without restriction. For Medicaid planning, treat them carefully and seek guidance. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to coordinate your benefits.

faqs.q: Do I have to be completely unable to leave home?
faqs.a: Not entirely. The standard is being substantially confined to your home or immediate premises due to a permanent disability. Occasional medical trips do not disqualify you. The VA looks at whether your disability generally keeps you home. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand how the rule applies to you.

page.metaTitle: VA Aid and Attendance Eligibility Rules | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: VA Aid and Attendance eligibility depends on wartime service, care needs, and income limits. See if you qualify in 2026. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find out.
page.quickAnswer: VA Aid and Attendance eligibility requires 90+ days of active duty with at least one day during a wartime period, an other-than-dishonorable discharge, a demonstrated care need, and income and net-worth within VA limits. Surviving spouses of qualifying veterans may also be eligible, per the VA.
page.intro: Wondering if you qualify? Here are the four pillars of VA Aid and Attendance eligibility in 2026.

sections.h2: The service requirement
sections.body: To be eligible, a veteran must generally have served at least 90 days of active duty, with at least one of those days falling during a VA-recognized wartime period such as World War II, Korea, Vietnam, or the Gulf War era. A discharge under conditions other than dishonorable is required. Combat service is not necessary; stateside or support roles during wartime can still satisfy the rule. Surviving spouses qualify based on the deceased veteran's service. If you are unsure whether your service dates fall within a wartime window, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and a licensed advisor can check for you.

sections.h2: The care-need and financial requirements
sections.body: Beyond service, you must show a medical need, such as requiring help with bathing, dressing, eating, or medications, being bedridden, living in a nursing home, or having severely limited eyesight. You must also meet the VA's income and net-worth limits. Importantly, the VA subtracts unreimbursed medical and care expenses from your income, so many seniors with significant care costs qualify even with moderate income. A 36-month look-back period reviews asset transfers. Because these rules interact, professional guidance helps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see where you stand.

faqs.q: What wartime periods qualify for Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: VA-recognized wartime periods include World War II, the Korean War, the Vietnam War era, and the Gulf War era, among others. You need at least one day of active duty during such a period, plus 90+ total days of service. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your dates qualify.

faqs.q: Is there an age requirement for Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: Pension applicants are generally eligible at age 65 or older, or at any age if permanently and totally disabled. Aid and Attendance itself is based on care need rather than age. Most recipients are seniors. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review your specific eligibility.

faqs.q: What disqualifies someone from Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: Common disqualifiers include insufficient wartime service, a dishonorable discharge, income or net worth above VA limits, or no qualifying care need. Asset transfers within the 36-month look-back can also create penalties. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to identify and address any obstacles before you apply.

faqs.q: Can I qualify if I have a pension or 401(k)?
faqs.a: Possibly. Retirement income counts toward the income test, but high unreimbursed medical and care costs are subtracted, which can bring countable income low enough to qualify. Net worth is also reviewed. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to run the numbers for your household.

faqs.q: Does a surviving spouse meet eligibility on their own service?
faqs.a: No. A surviving spouse qualifies based on the deceased veteran's wartime service, provided they were married at the time of death and have not remarried. The spouse must still meet care-need and financial limits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check surviving-spouse eligibility.

children.keyword: va aid and attendance income limits

page.metaTitle: VA Aid and Attendance Income Limits 2026 | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: VA Aid and Attendance income limits use the MAPR minus countable income, with medical costs deducted. Many qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check.
page.h1: VA Aid and Attendance Income Limits
page.quickAnswer: VA Aid and Attendance does not use a flat income cutoff. Your benefit equals the Maximum Annual Pension Rate (MAPR) minus your countable income, after subtracting unreimbursed medical and care expenses. Because care costs are deducted, many seniors with moderate income still qualify, per the VA.
page.intro: VA Aid and Attendance income limits work differently than most people expect. Here is how the math actually works in 2026.

sections.h2: How the income test really works
sections.body: The VA does not simply deny applicants who earn over a fixed number. Instead, it sets a Maximum Annual Pension Rate (MAPR) for your category, then subtracts your countable income to determine your benefit. Crucially, before that subtraction, the VA removes your unreimbursed medical and care expenses, such as assisted living fees, in-home aides, insurance premiums, and prescriptions, from your income. For many seniors paying thousands per month for care, these deductions push countable income to zero, qualifying them for the maximum benefit. This is why so many veterans who assume they earn too much actually qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to run the numbers.

sections.h2: Net worth limits also apply
sections.body: In addition to income, the VA applies a net-worth limit that combines your assets and annual income. The limit is tied to a federal figure that adjusts each year, and your primary home, vehicle, and personal belongings are generally excluded. A 36-month look-back period reviews asset transfers made for less than fair value, which can trigger a penalty period. Because net-worth and income rules interact, and because the right deductions can make the difference between approval and denial, expert guidance pays off. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand the current limits and how your finances measure up.

faqs.q: What is the income limit for Aid and Attendance in 2026?
faqs.a: There is no single cutoff. Your benefit equals the MAPR for your category minus countable income, after deducting unreimbursed medical and care costs. Those deductions often reduce countable income to zero. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for the current MAPR figures and a personalized estimate.

faqs.q: What counts as a deductible medical expense?
faqs.a: Deductible unreimbursed expenses include assisted living and nursing home fees, in-home caregiver costs, health and Medigap insurance premiums, prescriptions, and other out-of-pocket medical costs. These directly lower your countable income. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn which of your expenses qualify.

faqs.q: Does Social Security count as income?
faqs.a: Yes. Social Security is countable income, but your unreimbursed medical and care expenses are subtracted from your total income before the benefit is calculated. So Social Security alone rarely disqualifies an applicant with real care costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to estimate your benefit.

faqs.q: What is the net worth limit for Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: The VA uses a combined net-worth limit that includes assets plus annual income and adjusts each year. Your home, vehicle, and personal items are generally excluded. A 36-month look-back reviews transfers. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for the current limit and net-worth planning guidance.

children.keyword: how to apply for va aid and attendance

page.metaTitle: How to Apply for VA Aid and Attendance | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Apply for VA Aid and Attendance with the right forms and documents to avoid delays. Step-by-step guide for seniors. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.
page.h1: How to Apply for VA Aid and Attendance
page.quickAnswer: To apply for VA Aid and Attendance, submit your VA pension application (VA Form 21P-527EZ for veterans or 21P-534EZ for survivors) along with VA Form 21-2680, your DD-214, and medical and financial records. You can file online, by mail, or with a VA-accredited representative, per VA.gov.
page.intro: Applying for VA Aid and Attendance is straightforward when you have the right documents. Follow these steps to avoid common delays.

sections.h2: Step 1: Gather your documents
sections.body: Before you file, collect your DD-214 or other discharge papers proving wartime service, a marriage certificate (and death certificate for surviving spouses), proof of income and assets, and detailed medical documentation of your care needs. Have your doctor complete VA Form 21-2680, which certifies your need for aid and attendance or housebound status. If you live in assisted living or use in-home care, gather invoices showing those costs, since they reduce your countable income. Missing or incomplete documents are the top cause of delays. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can give you a checklist tailored to your situation.

sections.h2: Step 2: File the application
sections.body: Veterans file VA Form 21P-527EZ and surviving spouses file VA Form 21P-534EZ, attaching VA Form 21-2680 and your supporting records. You can submit online at VA.gov, mail the package to the VA Pension Management Center, or work with a VA-accredited representative or Veterans Service Organization, which can file on your behalf at no cost. After filing, the VA may schedule an exam or request more information. Applicants age 90 or older can request expedited processing. For a no-pressure walkthrough that helps you file correctly the first time, call 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free for all things senior in America.

faqs.q: What forms do I need to apply for Aid and Attendance?
faqs.a: Veterans use VA Form 21P-527EZ and surviving spouses use VA Form 21P-534EZ, both filed with VA Form 21-2680 certifying your care need. Include your DD-214 and financial and medical records. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP if you need help locating or completing the forms.

faqs.q: Can I apply for Aid and Attendance online?
faqs.a: Yes. You can file your pension and Aid and Attendance application online at VA.gov, by mail to the Pension Management Center, or in person with a VA-accredited representative. Filing accurately matters more than the method. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance on the best route for you.

faqs.q: How long does the application take to process?
faqs.a: Many Aid and Attendance claims take several months to decide, though times vary. Veterans 90 or older or facing hardship can request expedited review. Approved benefits are generally paid back to your claim's effective date. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to start your application without costly mistakes.

faqs.q: Do I have to pay someone to apply?
faqs.a: No. VA-accredited representatives and Veterans Service Organizations help file claims for free, and charging for initial claim preparation is restricted by law. Be cautious of anyone demanding large upfront fees. For trustworthy, no-pressure help, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What happens after I submit my application?
faqs.a: The VA reviews your documents and may request a medical exam or additional records. You will receive a decision letter explaining your award or denial, with appeal rights if denied. Keep copies of everything you submit. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP if you need help responding to a VA request.

page.metaTitle: VA Aid and Attendance for Assisted Living | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: VA Aid and Attendance can help pay assisted living costs for veterans and spouses. Fees count as deductible expenses. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how.
page.h1: VA Aid and Attendance for Assisted Living
page.quickAnswer: VA Aid and Attendance can help pay for assisted living because monthly facility fees count as unreimbursed medical expenses that lower your countable income and increase your benefit. The tax-free cash can be applied directly to assisted living costs, per the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs.
page.intro: Assisted living is expensive, but VA Aid and Attendance can help eligible veterans and surviving spouses afford it. Here is how.

sections.h2: How Aid and Attendance helps with assisted living costs
sections.body: Assisted living can cost several thousand dollars per month, and Medicare does not cover it. VA Aid and Attendance helps in two ways. First, because your benefit is paid as tax-free cash, you can apply it directly to your monthly facility bill. Second, those facility fees count as unreimbursed medical expenses, which the VA subtracts from your income, often pushing your countable income low enough to qualify for the maximum benefit. In other words, the very cost of assisted living can both raise your benefit and be paid by it. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see how the math works for your community.

sections.h2: Qualifying when you live in assisted living
sections.body: Living in an assisted living community often strengthens an Aid and Attendance claim because the care you receive helps satisfy the medical-need requirement. To document this, ask the facility for a statement of the care services you receive and have your physician complete VA Form 21-2680. Keep monthly invoices showing your fees, since these support both your care need and your expense deductions. Pair this with proof of wartime service and your financial records. Because facilities vary in how they document care, getting it right matters. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help assembling a strong application.

faqs.q: Does Aid and Attendance pay the full cost of assisted living?
faqs.a: Not usually. Aid and Attendance provides a meaningful monthly amount, but it is designed to help offset, not fully cover, assisted living costs. Most families combine it with Social Security and other income. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see how much the benefit could contribute toward your community's fees.

faqs.q: Do assisted living fees count as medical expenses?
faqs.a: Yes. When you live in assisted living for needed care, the VA generally counts your facility fees as unreimbursed medical expenses, which lower your countable income and can raise your benefit. Keep monthly invoices as proof. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn which costs qualify.

faqs.q: Can memory care or dementia care qualify?
faqs.a: Yes. Memory and dementia care communities provide help with daily activities and supervision that typically meet the Aid and Attendance care-need standard, and their fees count as medical expenses. Documentation from the facility and physician is key. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help with a memory care claim.

faqs.q: What if my spouse needs assisted living but I do not?
faqs.a: A veteran whose spouse needs care, or a surviving spouse who needs care, may still qualify depending on the household situation. The care need and expenses are evaluated against the applicable rate. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review how your family's circumstances affect eligibility.

page.metaDescription: The VA pension gives low-income wartime veterans a tax-free monthly benefit, with add-ons for care. See eligibility and rates. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: The VA pension is a tax-free monthly benefit for low-income wartime veterans age 65 or older, or permanently disabled, who served at least 90 days of active duty with one day during a wartime period. Aid and Attendance and Housebound benefits can increase the amount, per VA.gov.
page.intro: The VA pension is the foundation of needs-based veteran benefits. Here is who qualifies and how add-on benefits boost it.

sections.h2: What is the VA pension?
sections.body: The VA pension, sometimes called the Veterans Pension, is a needs-based, tax-free monthly payment for wartime veterans with limited income and net worth. It is separate from VA disability compensation, which is service-connected. To qualify, a veteran must have served at least 90 days of active duty with at least one day during a VA-recognized wartime period, received an other-than-dishonorable discharge, and be age 65 or older or permanently and totally disabled. The pension brings a veteran's income up to a set level called the Maximum Annual Pension Rate. Veterans who need care can add Aid and Attendance or Housebound benefits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn your options.

sections.h2: How the pension and care add-ons work together
sections.body: The basic pension is the starting point, but most senior veterans who need help qualify for more. Aid and Attendance adds money for those needing help with daily activities or living in a care facility, while Housebound adds money for those confined to home by disability. You cannot receive both add-ons at once and should claim whichever pays more. Your actual payment equals the MAPR for your category minus your countable income, with unreimbursed medical costs subtracted first. This structure rewards documenting your real care expenses. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to maximize the pension you are entitled to.

faqs.q: Who is eligible for the VA pension?
faqs.a: Wartime veterans with at least 90 days of active duty including one wartime day, an other-than-dishonorable discharge, and limited income and net worth qualify if they are 65 or older or permanently disabled. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm whether you meet the requirements.

faqs.q: Is the VA pension the same as VA disability?
faqs.a: No. The VA pension is needs-based and tied to wartime service and income, while disability compensation is for service-connected conditions and is not income-based. A veteran generally claims whichever pays more. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare your options.

faqs.q: How much is the VA pension in 2026?
faqs.a: The pension equals the Maximum Annual Pension Rate for your category minus countable income, paid monthly. Aid and Attendance and Housebound add-ons raise the maximum. Rates adjust each year with cost-of-living increases. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for the current figures and an estimate.

faqs.q: Can wartime veterans add Aid and Attendance to their pension?
faqs.a: Yes. Veterans who need help with daily activities, live in a care facility, or are bedridden can add Aid and Attendance to their basic pension for a higher monthly amount. You must document the care need. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to apply for the add-on.

faqs.q: Does the VA pension affect Social Security?
faqs.a: No. The VA pension does not reduce your Social Security, though Social Security counts as income when the VA calculates your pension amount. Your unreimbursed medical costs are deducted first. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand how the two benefits work together.

page.metaDescription: Veterans over 65 may qualify for VA pension, Aid and Attendance, health care, and more. See your options and how they pair with Medicare. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Veterans over 65 may qualify for the VA pension, Aid and Attendance, Housebound benefits, VA health care, and long-term care services. These pair with Medicare and a Medigap plan to cut out-of-pocket costs. Eligibility depends on service, income, and care needs, per VA.gov.
page.intro: Turning 65 opens new VA benefit options for senior veterans. Here is an overview of what may be available to you.

sections.h2: Key VA benefits for senior veterans
sections.body: Veterans over 65 have several benefits worth reviewing. The VA pension provides tax-free monthly income for low-income wartime veterans, and the Aid and Attendance and Housebound add-ons increase it for those who need care. VA health care offers comprehensive medical coverage, often with low or no copays depending on priority group. Long-term care services include in-home care, adult day programs, nursing homes, and community living centers. Many senior veterans also keep Medicare and a Medigap plan for flexibility. Knowing which benefits you qualify for, and how they combine, can save thousands. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to map out your options.

sections.h2: Combining VA benefits with Medicare
sections.body: Most veterans over 65 do not have to choose between VA benefits and Medicare; they use both. VA health care covers care at VA facilities, while Medicare and a Medigap plan cover care from civilian doctors and hospitals nationwide. Keeping Medicare gives you access to providers outside the VA system and protects you if VA eligibility or facility access changes. Pension benefits like Aid and Attendance then provide cash for long-term care that neither program fully covers. As the trusted toll-free for all things senior in America, 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you align VA benefits, Medicare, and Medigap in one call.

faqs.q: What VA benefits can I get at 65?
faqs.a: At 65, veterans may qualify for the VA pension, Aid and Attendance, Housebound benefits, VA health care, and long-term care services, depending on service and income. Many also keep Medicare and Medigap. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review which benefits fit you.

faqs.q: Should veterans over 65 keep Medicare?
faqs.a: Generally yes. Keeping Medicare and a Medigap plan gives you access to civilian doctors and hospitals nationwide and protects you if VA access changes. VA and Medicare work well together. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to coordinate your coverage for the lowest out-of-pocket costs.

faqs.q: Does the VA offer long-term care for seniors?
faqs.a: Yes. The VA offers long-term care including in-home care, adult day health care, nursing homes, and community living centers, though availability and cost depend on eligibility and priority group. Aid and Attendance can help pay private care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to explore options.

faqs.q: Do I need a service-connected disability to get VA benefits at 65?
faqs.a: No. Needs-based benefits like the VA pension and Aid and Attendance do not require a service-connected disability; they require wartime service and limited income. VA health care eligibility also extends beyond service-connected veterans. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check your eligibility.

faqs.q: Can my spouse get VA benefits too?
faqs.a: Possibly. Spouses and surviving spouses may qualify for survivor pensions, Aid and Attendance, and certain health programs based on the veteran's service. Eligibility rules vary by program. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn what your spouse may be entitled to.

page.metaDescription: VA Aid and Attendance 2026 rates use the MAPR by category, adjusted for income and COLA. Get current figures for your situation. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: VA Aid and Attendance 2026 rates are set by Maximum Annual Pension Rates (MAPR) that differ by category: single veteran, married veteran, two-veteran couple, and surviving spouse. Rates rise each year with cost-of-living adjustments, and your benefit equals the MAPR minus countable income, per VA.gov.
page.intro: VA Aid and Attendance rates change each year. Here is how the 2026 figures are structured and what they mean for your benefit.

sections.h2: How 2026 rates are structured
sections.body: The VA sets Aid and Attendance amounts through Maximum Annual Pension Rates that vary by household category. A single veteran has one MAPR, a married veteran (or veteran with a dependent) has a higher one, two married veterans have the highest, and a surviving spouse has a lower tier. Each year these rates increase by the same cost-of-living adjustment applied to Social Security. Your actual benefit is not the full MAPR; it equals the MAPR for your category minus your countable income, divided by 12 for a monthly payment. Because exact dollar figures update annually, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for the current 2026 amounts and a personalized estimate.

sections.h2: Why your check may differ from the maximum
sections.body: Two veterans in the same category can receive very different checks because the benefit is reduced by countable income. The key lever is the deduction for unreimbursed medical and care expenses, which the VA subtracts from your income before calculating your payment. A veteran with high care costs may have little or no countable income and therefore receive close to the maximum, while one with low expenses receives less. This is why documenting every eligible medical and care cost matters so much. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to estimate your 2026 benefit based on your actual income and expenses.

faqs.q: How much is Aid and Attendance per month in 2026?
faqs.a: It depends on your category and income. A married veteran can receive more than a single veteran, and a surviving spouse less. Your payment equals the 2026 MAPR minus countable income, divided by 12. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for the current monthly figures and your estimate.

faqs.q: Do Aid and Attendance rates increase each year?
faqs.a: Yes. The VA applies the same annual cost-of-living adjustment used for Social Security, so the Maximum Annual Pension Rates rise most years. The 2026 rates reflect the latest COLA. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for the exact current amounts.

faqs.q: What are the different rate categories?
faqs.a: The main categories are single veteran, married veteran or veteran with a dependent, two married veterans, and surviving spouse. Each has its own Maximum Annual Pension Rate, with two-veteran couples highest and surviving spouses lowest. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find your category and rate.

faqs.q: Why is my benefit less than the maximum rate?
faqs.a: Your benefit equals the MAPR minus your countable income. If you have countable income after deducting medical and care costs, your check is reduced accordingly. Higher documented care expenses mean a larger benefit. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to maximize your payment.

children.keyword: va nursing home benefits for veterans

page.metaTitle: VA Nursing Home Benefits for Veterans | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: VA nursing home benefits include community living centers, contract care, and state veterans homes, plus Aid and Attendance cash. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: VA Nursing Home Benefits for Veterans
page.quickAnswer: VA nursing home benefits include VA Community Living Centers, community nursing homes under VA contract, and state veterans homes. Eligibility depends on service-connected status, level of care needed, and priority. Aid and Attendance pension can also help pay private nursing home costs, per VA.gov.
page.intro: The VA offers several nursing home options for veterans who need round-the-clock care. Here is how they work.

sections.h2: Types of VA nursing home care
sections.body: The VA provides nursing home care through three main avenues. Community Living Centers are VA-operated facilities offering short- and long-term skilled care. Community nursing homes are private facilities the VA contracts with, allowing veterans to receive care closer to home. State veterans homes are owned and operated by states with VA support and grants. Eligibility and cost depend on your service-connected disability status, clinical need, and priority group; veterans with higher service-connected ratings often receive care at little or no cost. For those using private nursing homes, Aid and Attendance pension can help cover the bill. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand which option fits.

sections.h2: How nursing home benefits work with Medicare and pension
sections.body: VA nursing home benefits, Medicare, and the VA pension can work together. Medicare covers limited short-term skilled nursing care after a qualifying hospital stay but does not pay for long-term custodial care. The VA may cover nursing home care based on service connection and need, while Aid and Attendance provides tax-free cash that can be applied to a private nursing home when VA coverage is limited or unavailable. Coordinating these correctly prevents gaps and unexpected bills. As the trusted toll-free for all things senior in America, 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you align VA nursing home benefits, Medicare, and pension.

faqs.q: Does the VA pay for nursing home care?
faqs.a: The VA may pay for nursing home care through Community Living Centers, contracted community nursing homes, and state veterans homes, depending on your service-connected status, clinical need, and priority group. Veterans with higher disability ratings often pay little. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check your eligibility.

faqs.q: Can Aid and Attendance pay for a nursing home?
faqs.a: Yes. Aid and Attendance provides tax-free cash that can help pay for a private nursing home, and nursing home fees count as deductible medical expenses that raise your benefit. This helps when VA-covered care is limited. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how to combine them.

faqs.q: What is a state veterans home?
faqs.a: A state veterans home is a nursing or care facility owned and run by a state with federal VA support. Each state sets its own admission rules, often prioritizing veterans and sometimes spouses. Costs and availability vary. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to explore state veterans homes near you.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover long-term nursing home care?
faqs.a: No. Medicare covers only limited short-term skilled nursing care after a qualifying hospital stay, not long-term custodial care. VA benefits and Aid and Attendance help fill that gap for veterans. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to coordinate Medicare, Medigap, and VA nursing home benefits.

page.metaDescription: VA pension eligibility for seniors requires wartime service, age 65+ or disability, and income within limits. See if you qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: VA pension eligibility for seniors requires 90+ days of active duty with one day during a wartime period, an other-than-dishonorable discharge, age 65 or older (or permanent disability), and income and net worth within VA limits. Surviving spouses may also qualify, per VA.gov.
page.intro: Many senior veterans qualify for the VA pension without realizing it. Here is a clear breakdown of the eligibility rules.

sections.h2: Service and age requirements
sections.body: To be eligible for the VA pension as a senior, a veteran must generally have served at least 90 days of active duty with at least one day during a VA-recognized wartime period and received a discharge other than dishonorable. Veterans who entered active duty after September 7, 1980, may face a longer minimum service requirement. You must be age 65 or older, or under 65 but permanently and totally disabled. Combat is not required; wartime-era service in any role can count. Surviving spouses qualify based on the veteran's service. If you are unsure whether your dates qualify, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and a licensed advisor will check.

sections.h2: Income and net worth limits
sections.body: Beyond service and age, the VA pension is needs-based, so your income and net worth must fall within annual limits. The net-worth limit combines assets and income and adjusts each year, with your home, vehicle, and personal belongings generally excluded. Importantly, the VA subtracts unreimbursed medical and care expenses from your income, so many seniors with significant care costs qualify even with moderate income. A 36-month look-back period reviews asset transfers for less than fair value. Because the financial rules are nuanced, professional help often makes the difference. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see whether you qualify.

faqs.q: What age qualifies a senior for the VA pension?
faqs.a: Veterans are generally eligible at age 65 or older. Those under 65 may qualify if they are permanently and totally disabled. The pension is needs-based, so income and net worth also matter. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your eligibility at your age.

faqs.q: How much military service is required for the pension?
faqs.a: You generally need at least 90 days of active duty with at least one day during a wartime period. Veterans who started service after September 7, 1980, may need a longer minimum. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to verify whether your service meets the requirement.

faqs.q: Can a senior with savings still qualify?
faqs.a: Possibly. The VA uses a combined net-worth limit that adjusts annually, and your home and vehicle are usually excluded. High unreimbursed medical costs also reduce countable income. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review your assets and income against the current limits.

faqs.q: Do surviving spouses qualify for the VA pension?
faqs.a: Yes. A surviving spouse of a qualifying wartime veteran may receive a Survivors Pension if they were married at the time of death, have not remarried, and meet income and net-worth limits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check surviving-spouse eligibility.

faqs.q: Does the wartime service have to be overseas?
faqs.a: No. Service does not have to be overseas or in combat. You only need at least one day of active duty during a VA-recognized wartime period, plus the required total days of service. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your service dates qualify.

children.keyword: va health care eligibility for elderly veterans

page.metaTitle: VA Health Care Eligibility for Elderly Veterans | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: VA health care eligibility for elderly veterans depends on service, income, and priority group. See how it pairs with Medicare. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: VA Health Care Eligibility for Elderly Veterans
page.quickAnswer: VA health care eligibility for elderly veterans depends on length and character of service, service-connected disabilities, income, and assigned priority group. Most veterans who served the minimum active-duty period with an other-than-dishonorable discharge can enroll, and many pair VA care with Medicare, per VA.gov.
page.intro: VA health care offers elderly veterans comprehensive coverage. Here is how eligibility and priority groups work.

sections.h2: How VA health care eligibility works
sections.body: Eligibility for VA health care generally requires service in the active military with a discharge other than dishonorable, and meeting minimum duty-length requirements for those who enlisted after September 7, 1980. When you enroll, the VA assigns you to one of eight priority groups based on factors such as service-connected disability rating, income, former prisoner-of-war status, and special honors like the Purple Heart or Medal of Honor. Your priority group affects your copays and how quickly you can enroll. Veterans with service-connected conditions or lower incomes typically pay little or nothing. Eligibility rules have expanded in recent years. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand your priority group.

sections.h2: Pairing VA health care with Medicare
sections.body: Elderly veterans enrolled in VA health care can also keep Medicare, and most should. VA health care covers services at VA facilities, while Medicare and a Medigap plan cover civilian doctors and hospitals nationwide. Having both protects you if a VA facility is far away, has long waits, or does not offer a needed service. It also preserves your choice of providers. The VA itself recommends that eligible veterans enroll in Medicare when first eligible to avoid late-enrollment penalties later. As the trusted toll-free for all things senior in America, 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you coordinate VA health care with Medicare and Medigap.

faqs.q: Can elderly veterans enroll in VA health care at any time?
faqs.a: Yes. VA health care has no annual enrollment window; eligible veterans can apply at any time online, by phone, or in person. Once enrolled, you are assigned a priority group that determines copays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help understanding your eligibility and enrollment.

faqs.q: Do elderly veterans need Medicare if they have VA care?
faqs.a: It is strongly recommended. VA care covers VA facilities, but Medicare with Medigap covers civilian providers nationwide and protects you from gaps and late-enrollment penalties. Most veterans keep both. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to coordinate VA care with Medicare.

faqs.q: What are VA priority groups?
faqs.a: Priority groups are eight categories the VA uses to manage enrollment and copays, based on service-connected disability, income, and special status like Purple Heart recipients. Lower group numbers receive higher priority and lower costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn your likely priority group.

faqs.q: Does income affect VA health care eligibility?
faqs.a: It can. Income helps determine your priority group and copays, though many veterans qualify regardless of income, especially those with service-connected conditions. Eligibility has expanded in recent years. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review how your income affects your VA health care.

faqs.q: Does VA health care cover prescriptions for seniors?
faqs.a: Yes. Enrolled veterans can receive prescription coverage through the VA, often with low copays based on priority group. Some seniors compare this with Medicare Part D. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to weigh VA prescription coverage against your Medicare drug options.

page.metaDescription: Elderly veterans benefits programs include pension, Aid and Attendance, health care, and long-term care. See your options in one place. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Elderly veterans benefits programs include the VA pension, Aid and Attendance and Housebound add-ons, VA health care, long-term and geriatric care, survivor benefits, and burial benefits. Most pair with Medicare and Medigap to reduce out-of-pocket costs. Eligibility varies by program, per VA.gov.
page.intro: There are many VA programs designed for senior veterans and their families. Here is an overview of the most valuable ones.

sections.h2: Core benefit programs for elderly veterans
sections.body: Several VA programs serve senior veterans. The VA pension provides tax-free monthly income for low-income wartime veterans, and the Aid and Attendance and Housebound add-ons increase it for those who need care. VA health care delivers comprehensive medical coverage through priority groups. Geriatrics and extended care programs cover in-home care, adult day health care, respite care, nursing homes, and community living centers. Survivor benefits such as the Survivors Pension and Dependency and Indemnity Compensation support spouses. Burial and memorial benefits help families at end of life. Knowing which programs you qualify for can save thousands. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review them all.

sections.h2: How these programs work with Medicare and Medigap
sections.body: VA programs are most powerful when coordinated with Medicare and a Medigap plan. VA health care covers VA facilities, Medicare and Medigap cover civilian providers nationwide, and pension benefits like Aid and Attendance provide cash for long-term care that medical insurance does not cover. Together they form a safety net that few veterans fully use. The challenge is that these programs have different rules, forms, and offices, which is why many families miss benefits they have earned. As the trusted toll-free for all things senior in America, 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you understand every program and align it with the right Medicare coverage in one call.

faqs.q: What benefit programs are available to elderly veterans?
faqs.a: Elderly veterans may access the VA pension, Aid and Attendance, Housebound benefits, VA health care, geriatric and long-term care, survivor benefits, and burial benefits. Eligibility varies by program and service history. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find out which programs you qualify for.

faqs.q: Are there VA programs that help veterans stay at home?
faqs.a: Yes. The VA offers home-based primary care, homemaker and home health aide services, adult day health care, respite care, and the Aid and Attendance pension, all of which can help veterans remain at home. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to explore in-home care options.

faqs.q: Do these programs help surviving spouses?
faqs.a: Yes. Survivor programs include the Survivors Pension with Aid and Attendance, Dependency and Indemnity Compensation, and burial benefits. Eligibility depends on the veteran's service and the spouse's circumstances. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn what a surviving spouse may receive.

faqs.q: How do I find out which programs I qualify for?
faqs.a: Because VA programs have different rules and offices, the easiest way is to review your service, income, and care needs with someone who knows the system. A VA-accredited representative or Veterans Service Organization can help for free. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a no-pressure overview of your options.

faqs.q: Can elderly veterans use VA benefits and Medicare together?
faqs.a: Yes, and most should. VA benefits cover VA facilities while Medicare and Medigap cover civilian providers nationwide, and pension benefits add cash for long-term care. Together they minimize out-of-pocket costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to coordinate all your coverage in one call.
